content.text: Azure Integration Services is the family of managed services for connecting applications, data, and systems both inside Azure and with external partners and SaaS platforms. The four core services — Service Bus, Logic Apps, API Management, and Event Grid — address distinct integration patterns: reliable messaging, workflow automation, API gateway, and event routing. They are designed to be composable: a typical enterprise integration scenario chains two or three of them together.

Azure Service Bus is a fully managed enterprise message broker that implements the AMQP 1.0 protocol. It provides two messaging entities: queues (point-to-point, competing consumers) and topics with subscriptions (publish-subscribe, fan-out). Service Bus guarantees at-least-once delivery, ordered delivery within sessions, and dead-letter queuing for unprocessable messages.
SQL filters or correlation filters per subscription
Yes — ordered delivery per session ID

Service Bus sessions enable message ordering per entity (e.g., per customer or per order). A session-aware receiver locks all messages with the same sessionId, ensuring sequential processing. Without sessions, competing consumers process messages out of order.

# Create a Service Bus namespace (Standard tier supports topics)
az servicebus namespace create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myServiceBusNS \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard

# Create a queue with dead-letter and duplicate detection
az servicebus queue create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --namespace-name myServiceBusNS \
  --name orders \
  --enable-dead-lettering-on-message-expiration true \
  --duplicate-detection-history-time-window PT10M \
  --max-delivery-count 5 \
  --default-message-time-to-live P7D

# Create a topic with two subscriptions (fan-out)
az servicebus topic create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --namespace-name myServiceBusNS \
  --name events

az servicebus topic subscription create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --namespace-name myServiceBusNS \
  --topic-name events \
  --name email-service

az servicebus topic subscription create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --namespace-name myServiceBusNS \
  --topic-name events \
  --name audit-log

# Add a SQL filter: email-service only receives OrderPlaced events
az servicebus topic subscription rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --namespace-name myServiceBusNS \
  --topic-name events \
  --subscription-name email-service \
  --name OrderPlacedFilter \
  --filter-sql-expression "eventType = 'OrderPlaced'"

# Get connection string for a namespace
az servicebus namespace authorization-rule keys list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --namespace-name myServiceBusNS \
  --name RootManageSharedAccessKey \
  --query primaryConnectionString \
  --output tsv

# Send and receive messages using azure-servicebus SDK
from azure.servicebus import ServiceBusClient, ServiceBusMessage

conn_str = 'Endpoint=sb://myservicebusns.servicebus.windows.net/;...'
queue_name = 'orders'

# Send a batch of messages
with ServiceBusClient.from_connection_string(conn_str) as client:
    with client.get_queue_sender(queue_name) as sender:
        batch = sender.create_message_batch()
        for order_id in range(1, 11):
            batch.add_message(ServiceBusMessage(
                body=f'Order {order_id}',
                application_properties={'orderId': order_id, 'priority': 'high'},
                session_id=str(order_id % 3)  # group by modulo for session ordering
            ))
        sender.send_messages(batch)
        print('Sent 10 messages')

# Receive and complete messages (removes from queue)
with ServiceBusClient.from_connection_string(conn_str) as client:
    with client.get_queue_receiver(queue_name, max_wait_time=5) as receiver:
        for msg in receiver:
            try:
                print(f'Processing: {str(msg)}')
                # Business logic here
                receiver.complete_message(msg)  # removes from queue
            except Exception as e:
                receiver.dead_letter_message(msg, reason=str(e))  # send to DLQ

# Process dead-letter queue
with ServiceBusClient.from_connection_string(conn_str) as client:
    dlq_name = f'{queue_name}/$DeadLetterQueue'
    with client.get_queue_receiver(dlq_name) as receiver:
        for msg in receiver:
            print(f'DLQ: {str(msg)}, reason: {msg.dead_letter_reason}')
            receiver.complete_message(msg)

Logic Apps provides visual workflow automation with 400+ connectors to Azure services, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, SAP, and other SaaS platforms. Workflows are defined as JSON (the workflow definition language) but are authored visually in the Azure portal or VS Code. Logic Apps comes in two hosting models with different trade-offs.
Pay per vCPU + memory (App Service plan)
Yes — outbound and inbound VNet integration
Both — stateless reduces latency and cost

# Create a Logic Apps Standard (VNet-integrated, single-tenant)
az logicapp create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myLogicApp \
  --storage-account myStorageAcct \
  --plan myASP \
  --location eastus

# List workflows in a Standard Logic App
az logicapp workflow list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myLogicApp

# View run history for a workflow
az logicapp workflow run list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myLogicApp \
  --workflow-name ProcessOrder \
  --query "[].{runId:name, status:properties.status, startTime:properties.startTime}" \
  --output table

# For Consumption Logic Apps: create and list
az logic workflow create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myConsumptionApp \
  --location eastus \
  --definition @workflow-definition.json

az logic workflow list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query "[].{name:name, state:properties.state, endpointUrl:properties.endpointConfiguration}" \
  --output table

# View trigger history (useful for HTTP-triggered workflows)
az logic workflow trigger history list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --workflow-name myConsumptionApp \
  --trigger-name manual

Azure API Management (APIM) is a fully managed API gateway that sits in front of backend services (Azure Functions, App Service, AKS, on-premises APIs, or any HTTPS endpoint). It decouples API consumers from backend implementations and applies cross-cutting concerns — authentication, authorization, rate limiting, caching, request/response transformation, and observability — through a declarative XML policy engine.

# Create an API Management service (Developer tier for non-prod)
az apim create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAPIM \
  --publisher-name 'Contoso' \
  --publisher-email admin@contoso.com \
  --sku-name Developer \
  --location eastus

# Import an API from an OpenAPI spec
az apim api import \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --service-name myAPIM \
  --api-id orders-api \
  --path orders \
  --specification-format OpenApi \
  --specification-url https://petstore.swagger.io/v2/swagger.json

# Add a named value (secret) for use in policies
az apim nv create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --service-name myAPIM \
  --named-value-id backend-api-key \
  --display-name backend-api-key \
  --value 'supersecret' \
  --secret true

# Create a product (groups APIs, controls access)
az apim product create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --service-name myAPIM \
  --product-id starter \
  --product-name Starter \
  --description 'Limited access tier' \
  --subscription-required true \
  --approval-required false \
  --state published

# Get the gateway URL and subscription key for testing
az apim show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAPIM \
  --query '{gatewayUrl:properties.gatewayUrl, devPortal:properties.developerPortalUrl}' \
  --output json

<!-- APIM inbound policy: validate JWT, rate-limit, cache, and transform request -->
<policies>
  <inbound>
    <base />
    <!-- Validate JWT from Azure AD -->
    <validate-jwt header-name="Authorization" failed-validation-httpcode="401"
                  failed-validation-error-message="Unauthorized">
      <openid-config url="https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenantId}/v2.0/.well-known/openid-configuration" />
      <required-claims>
        <claim name="aud" match="any">
          <value>api://myapp</value>
        </claim>
      </required-claims>
    </validate-jwt>
    <!-- Rate-limit: 10 calls per 60 seconds per subscription key -->
    <rate-limit calls="10" renewal-period="60" />
    <!-- Cache GET responses for 30 seconds -->
    <cache-lookup vary-by-developer="false" vary-by-developer-groups="false">
      <vary-by-query-parameter>category</vary-by-query-parameter>
    </cache-lookup>
    <!-- Forward caller IP to backend -->
    <set-header name="X-Forwarded-For" exists-action="override">
      <value>@(context.Request.IpAddress)</value>
    </set-header>
  </inbound>
  <backend>
    <base />
  </backend>
  <outbound>
    <cache-store duration="30" />
    <!-- Remove internal headers from response -->
    <set-header name="X-Powered-By" exists-action="delete" />
    <set-header name="Server" exists-action="delete" />
  </outbound>
  <on-error>
    <base />
  </on-error>
</policies>

Event Grid is a fully managed event routing service that delivers events from Azure resources (storage accounts, resource groups, Service Bus, IoT Hub, Container Registry, and others) and custom applications to handlers (webhooks, Azure Functions, Event Hubs, Service Bus queues, Logic Apps). Unlike Service Bus which is a message broker (pull-based, reliable queuing), Event Grid is a push-based event router optimized for near-real-time fan-out at low latency.
Built-in topics for Azure services (e.g., Microsoft.Storage.BlobCreated); created automatically
User-defined topic for publishing application events via HTTPS POST
Grouping of 100,000+ topics; useful for SaaS providers routing events to multiple customers
Route events from a topic to a handler endpoint; supports filtering by event type and subject
Undeliverable events routed to a Blob Storage container after retry exhaustion
Exponential back-off with jitter; max 24 hours, 30 attempts

# Create a custom Event Grid topic
az eventgrid topic create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myEventTopic \
  --location eastus \
  --input-schema cloudeventschemav1_0

# Subscribe to Blob Storage events (system topic) → send to Event Hub
STORAGE_ID=$(az storage account show -g myRG -n myStorageAcct --query id -o tsv)

az eventgrid event-subscription create \
  --source-resource-id $STORAGE_ID \
  --name blob-created-to-eventhub \
  --endpoint-type eventhub \
  --endpoint $(az eventhubs eventhub show -g myRG --namespace-name myEHNS -n myEH --query id -o tsv) \
  --included-event-types Microsoft.Storage.BlobCreated \
  --subject-begins-with /blobServices/default/containers/raw/

# Subscribe to custom topic → webhook (Azure Function)
TOPIC_ENDPOINT=$(az eventgrid topic show -g myRG -n myEventTopic --query endpoint -o tsv)
TOPIC_KEY=$(az eventgrid topic key list -g myRG -n myEventTopic --query key1 -o tsv)

az eventgrid event-subscription create \
  --source-resource-id $(az eventgrid topic show -g myRG -n myEventTopic --query id -o tsv) \
  --name process-order-events \
  --endpoint https://myfunctionapp.azurewebsites.net/api/ProcessOrder \
  --included-event-types OrderPlaced OrderCancelled \
  --deadletter-endpoint $(az storage account show -g myRG -n myStorageAcct --query id -o tsv)/blobServices/default/containers/deadletter

# Publish a CloudEvent to the custom topic
curl -X POST "$TOPIC_ENDPOINT/api/events" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/cloudevents+json" \
  -H "aeg-sas-key: $TOPIC_KEY" \
  -d '{
    "specversion": "1.0",
    "id": "order-001",
    "source": "/orders/api",
    "type": "OrderPlaced",
    "time": "2026-06-30T10:00:00Z",
    "data": {"orderId": "001", "amount": 99.99}
  }'
Decouple microservices: producer posts orders, consumers process asynchronously
Fan-out: one event triggers multiple independent downstream processors
Service Bus Topics or Event Grid
React to Azure resource events (blob created, VM deleted, etc.)
Automate multi-step workflow connecting SaaS apps without code
Enterprise workflow with VNet access to on-premises systems
Expose backend APIs to external partners with auth and rate limiting
B2B EDI: exchange AS2 or X12 messages with trading partners
Fan-out and filtering with SQL predicates on message properties
Event Hubs (not Event Grid or Service Bus)
Webhook validation and retry for reactive architectures

Azure integration services are typically combined in multi-service pipelines. Common architectural patterns include: API Management fronting Service Bus (synchronous API that queues work asynchronously), Event Grid triggering Logic Apps (react to resource events and orchestrate multi-step workflows), and Service Bus feeding Azure Functions (reliable, ordered processing with dead-letter handling for poison messages).

# Pattern: API Management → Service Bus (decouple API from backend)
# Policy fragment: APIM forwards POST /orders to Service Bus instead of backend
# (set this in the inbound policy of the POST /orders operation)

# Pattern: Event Grid → Logic App → Service Bus → Function (choreography)
# 1. Resource event fires → Event Grid
# 2. Event Grid delivers to Logic App webhook
# 3. Logic App transforms and puts message on Service Bus queue
# 4. Azure Function reads queue, processes message

# Monitoring: view Event Grid delivery failures
az monitor metrics list \
  --resource $(az eventgrid topic show -g myRG -n myEventTopic --query id -o tsv) \
  --metric 'DeliverySuccessCount' 'DeliveryFailureCount' 'DroppedEventCount' \
  --interval PT5M \
  --output table

# Check Service Bus queue depth (if depth grows, consumers are falling behind)
az servicebus queue show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --namespace-name myServiceBusNS \
  --name orders \
  --query 'countDetails.{active:activeMessageCount,dlq:deadLetterMessageCount,scheduled:scheduledMessageCount}'

# APIM: view API usage analytics
az apim api operation list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --service-name myAPIM \
  --api-id orders-api \
  --output table

# Logic Apps: resubmit failed runs
az logic workflow run action list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --workflow-name myConsumptionApp \
  --run-name 08585603648179239999 \
  --query "[?properties.status=='Failed'].{name:name, error:properties.error.message}"

Monitor queue depth (activeMessageCount) and dead-letter count continuously. A growing queue indicates consumers are slower than producers — scale out the consumer or increase throughput. A growing DLQ indicates message processing failures — inspect dead-letter reason and fix the consumer logic.
API Management Security and Advanced Configuration

Production API Management deployments require authentication, authorization, and network isolation. APIM supports OAuth 2.0 token validation (Azure AD, external IdPs), mutual TLS for backend authentication, client certificate validation, and managed identity for accessing backend secrets from Key Vault. For private APIs, APIM Premium can be deployed in Internal VNet mode — the gateway is only reachable from within the VNet, while the developer portal can be exposed through Application Gateway.

# Enable Application Insights for APIM diagnostics
APPINSIGHTS_ID=$(az monitor app-insights component show -g myRG --app myAppInsights --query id -o tsv)
APPINSIGHTS_KEY=$(az monitor app-insights component show -g myRG --app myAppInsights --query instrumentationKey -o tsv)

az apim logger create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --service-name myAPIM \
  --logger-id myAppInsightsLogger \
  --logger-type applicationInsights \
  --connection-string "InstrumentationKey=$APPINSIGHTS_KEY"

# Add a backend with circuit breaker (breaks after 5 failures in 60s)
az apim backend create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --service-name myAPIM \
  --backend-id orders-backend \
  --url https://orders.contoso.com \
  --protocol http

# Create a subscription for a product (developer gets API key)
az apim subscription create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --service-name myAPIM \
  --subscription-id dev-team-sub \
  --display-name 'Dev Team Subscription' \
  --scope /products/starter

# List all subscriptions and their keys
az apim subscription list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --service-name myAPIM \
  --query "[].{id:name, displayName:properties.displayName, state:properties.state}" \
  --output table

# Deploy APIM into a VNet (Premium tier, Internal mode — no public access)
az apim update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAPIM \
  --virtual-network Internal \
  --virtual-network-type Internal
Service Bus Security and Premium Features

Service Bus Premium tier offers dedicated capacity (no noisy-neighbor), 100 MB message size, private endpoints, and Geo-Disaster Recovery (Geo-DR). For security, Premium namespaces support managed identity authentication (no connection string in code), private endpoints, and local authentication can be disabled to force Azure AD-only access. Geo-DR pairs two Premium namespaces and fails over the alias (connection string) to the secondary without data replication — it is a metadata-only failover, not zero data loss.

# Premium namespace: private endpoint + managed identity access
az servicebus namespace create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myPremiumSBNS \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Premium \
  --capacity 1

# Assign Service Bus Data Sender role to an app's managed identity
APP_ID=$(az identity show -g myRG -n myAppIdentity --query principalId -o tsv)
SB_ID=$(az servicebus namespace show -g myRG -n myPremiumSBNS --query id -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --role 'Azure Service Bus Data Sender' \
  --assignee $APP_ID \
  --scope $SB_ID

az role assignment create \
  --role 'Azure Service Bus Data Receiver' \
  --assignee $APP_ID \
  --scope $SB_ID

# Disable local authentication (force managed identity/AAD only)
az servicebus namespace update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myPremiumSBNS \
  --disable-local-auth true

# Create private endpoint for Service Bus
az network private-endpoint create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name sbPrivateEndpoint \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --subnet AppSubnet \
  --private-connection-resource-id $SB_ID \
  --group-id namespace \
  --connection-name sb-connection

# Configure Geo-Disaster Recovery: pair two Premium namespaces
az servicebus georecovery-alias create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --namespace-name myPremiumSBNS \
  --alias myGeoAlias \
  --partner-namespace $(az servicebus namespace show -g myRG -n mySecondaryNS --query id -o tsv)

Logic Apps Standard workflows are deployed as ZIP packages to an App Service plan, enabling continuous deployment from GitHub Actions or Azure DevOps. Each Logic App resource can host multiple named workflows in separate subdirectories. Managed identity on the Logic App resource eliminates connection string management — the workflow authenticates to Service Bus, Key Vault, Storage, and other Azure services using its identity and Azure RBAC role assignments.

# Logic Apps Standard: deploy a workflow definition via Azure CLI
# The workflow definition goes in a folder structure:
# myLogicApp/
#   host.json
#   ProcessOrder/
#     workflow.json    <- the workflow definition JSON

# Package and deploy
cd myLogicApp && zip -r ../deploy.zip . && cd ..

az logicapp deployment source config-zip \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myLogicApp \
  --src deploy.zip

# View active workflow triggers (HTTP-triggered, recurring, etc.)
az logicapp workflow list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myLogicApp \
  --query "[].{workflow:name, state:properties.state}"

# Enable system-assigned managed identity for Logic App
az logicapp identity assign \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myLogicApp

# Grant Logic App access to Key Vault secrets
LOGIC_PRINCIPAL=$(az logicapp identity show -g myRG -n myLogicApp --query principalId -o tsv)
KV_ID=$(az keyvault show -g myRG -n myKeyVault --query id -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --role 'Key Vault Secrets User' \
  --assignee $LOGIC_PRINCIPAL \
  --scope $KV_ID

# Logic Apps: scale out Standard plan for high throughput
az appservice plan update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myASP \
  --number-of-workers 3
do not persist run history to storage; suitable for synchronous, low-latency operations. Restart after a crash replays from the trigger, so idempotency is required.
persist every action input/output to Azure Storage; enable run history inspection and resubmission from any step. Required for long-running or multi-day workflows.
by default, Logic Apps runs up to 25 concurrent trigger evaluations. Set trigger concurrency to 1 to serialize processing (e.g., when downstream systems cannot handle parallel calls).
use Scope actions with a Configure run after setting of 'has failed' to implement try/catch blocks within a workflow.

Azure Service Bus provides reliable, ordered enterprise messaging with queues (point-to-point) and topics/subscriptions (fan-out), AMQP 1.0, dead-letter queues, and sessions for ordered delivery. Azure Logic Apps offers visual workflow automation with 400+ connectors in two hosting models: Consumption (pay-per-execution, no VNet) and Standard (VNet-integrated, multiple workflows per resource). Azure API Management acts as an API gateway with a policy engine for JWT validation, rate limiting, caching, and request transformation, plus a developer portal and analytics. Azure Event Grid routes events from Azure resource system topics and custom application topics to handlers via push delivery with retry policies and dead-letter support. Use Service Bus for reliable asynchronous messaging, Logic Apps for workflow orchestration, APIM for API security and governance, and Event Grid for reactive event-driven architectures.
